narrative:
Brackenridge Civil Group is a small civil engineering practice in a
county seat, founded in 1996 and working for municipal public works
departments, a regional water authority, and two private developers. Its
work is site grading, stormwater, roadway, and utility design: drawings
go out under a sealed cover sheet, and the paperwork that surrounds them
is typed in Word and printed for the job file.

It is a firm of its era, and the file share shows it. Proposals and
design memoranda are Word documents saved to a lettered project folder.
Executed agreements come back countersigned by fax, get scanned on the
flatbed by the office administrator, and land in the share as crooked,
speckled images. Progress meetings with the county produce minutes that
list every attendee by full name and agency. The annual numbers live in
one spreadsheet the Principal Engineer keeps himself.

Writing is late-1990s American professional-services prose: complete
sentences, courteous but not warm, no buzzwords that would postdate the
document. Engineers refer to clients by agency name and to each other by
full name in anything that leaves the office. The Principal Engineer
signs every agreement and every letter to a public body. Nobody writes
"reach out"; they write "we will contact".
instructions:
Author every document briefed in `docs`, as JSON matching the deliverable
schema:

  {"schema_id": "orgsmith/authoring-deliverable@1",
   "work_order_id": "wo:author:0006",
   "docs": [{"schema_id": "orgsmith/docir@1", "doc_id": "d:....",
             "blocks": [...]}, ...]}

Block kinds: heading (text, level), paragraph (text), list (items),
table (header, rows), sigblock (signers = person ids from the brief).

Hard rules:
- One DocIR per brief; doc_ids must match the work order exactly.
- Every fact id briefed for a doc MUST appear as a literal placeholder
  {{fact:<id>}} at least once in that doc's text, where <id> is the
  briefed id verbatim including its `f:` prefix (e.g.
  {{fact:f:E-2019-001.start}}). You do not know fact values; never write
  a number, date, or name in place of a placeholder.
- Use only placeholders for fact ids briefed on that document.
- Do not invent people, organizations, addresses, amounts, or dates.
  People and their titles come from the briefs; the org narrative sets
  tone only.
- Every entry in a doc's `mentions` list must appear VERBATIM (the exact
  `surface` string) somewhere in that doc's text. Sigblock signers count
  as mentions of themselves. Short surfaces are nicknames: work them into
  prose naturally ("...as {{nickname}} noted..."), alongside, not instead
  of, the person's full name.
- If a doc carries `engagement_position`, honor it: it states where the
  document sits in its engagement's timeline. Do not describe progress or a
  phase inconsistent with it, and do not state the engagement's start or
  end date (those are fact placeholders, not yours to write).
- If a doc carries `firm_digest`, it is the ONLY firm history you may treat
  as established as of this document's date. Reference clients only through
  the placeholders it names; claim no engagement it does not list; present
  nothing later than this document as already true.
- Write plain, era-appropriate business prose in the org's voice.

docs.title: Engagement Letter: Utility Relocation Design for Franklin-Snyder

authors.name: Mark Todd
authors.title: Principal Engineer
authors.persona: Reads every agreement and every letter to a public body line by line before he signs it, and he signs all of them. His prose is formal and unhurried: complete sentences, the client named by agency, a courteous close that commits the firm to nothing it has not already agreed to do. He marks up drafts in red and returns them without much comment, on the theory that the correction is the comment. The firm's annual numbers are his own spreadsheet and he does not hand it to anyone.

participants.name: Linda George
participants.title: Project Engineer
participants.persona: Stormwater is her work and she writes about it plainly, leading with the conclusion and then showing how she got there. She runs progress meetings with public works staff and keeps her own notes even when minutes are being taken, because she has been burned by a recollection that differed from hers. Her memoranda are short, her drawings are not, and she would rather be asked a question than pad a document against one.

participants.name: Jason Jordan
participants.title: Design Technician
participants.persona: The one who knows the CAD standards, the plotting setup, and which layer somebody moved and should not have. Dry to the point of deadpan in the office, correct and colorless in anything typed. He is protective of the sealed cover sheet and treats a plan set going out the door as a thing that reflects on the firm, which it does.

docs.engagement_summary: Utility Relocation Design for Franklin-Snyder, running about 3 months. Commercial terms and dates are briefed as fact placeholders only.

facts.hint: amount of money; place where the figure belongs
calendar date; place where the date belongs
proper name; place where the name belongs

docs.guidance: Formal engagement letter on firm letterhead confirming the engagement. Do NOT repeat the firm's name as a heading or title line: the letterhead already carries it. Open with the client's inside address and a salutation, then cover scope and approach, the team, and the fee and start date, followed by the standard terms a real engagement letter carries -- each as its own short headed paragraph: fees and payment, term and termination, confidentiality, limitation of liability, and governing law. Close with a signature request and end with a sigblock block signed by the letter's author and the client contact.
docs.engagement_position: This document is dated about 0% of the way through the engagement (at or before kickoff); it runs roughly 3 months end to end. Do not describe progress inconsistent with that position, and do not state the engagement's start or end date.
